retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, neonatal respiratory distress syndrome was known as hyaline membrane disease, owing to an eosinophilic membrane that lines the distal airspaces, usually terminal bronchioles or alveolar ducts, in autopsies of neonates with RDS. Macroscopically, lung tissue from infants with RDS appears similar to hepatic tissue with a ruddy appearance. The hyaline membrane mentioned above is composed of fibrin, cellular debris from lung epithelium, red blood cells, and leukocytes. Microscopic histological examination may also reveal pulmonary tissue with few dilated alveoli among diffuse areas of atelectasis. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary pulmonary hypoplasia arises from extrinsic factors leading to fetal lung compression or reduced amniotic fluid volume. Commonly implicated are intrathoracic or intraabdominal space-occupying lesions, such as congenital diaphragmatic hernia and congenital pulmonary airway malformations, which disrupt normal lung development, leading to cystic and/or adenomatous pulmonary areas. [6] Chest wall deformities, including skeletal dysplasia, an inherited disorder of cartilage and/or bone that interferes with fetal lung development before 16 weeks of gestation, affect their growth. [7] In contrast, oligohydramnios caused by renal anomalies (eg, renal agenesis, Potter syndrome), urinary outflow tract obstruction, or prolonged premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) after 16 weeks gestation compromises lung maturation. [8] PPROM before 25 weeks with severe oligohydramnios significantly increases the risk of fatal pulmonary hypoplasia. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The key histologic changes in ARDS reveal the presence of alveolar edema in diseased lung areas. The type I pneumocytes and vascular endothelium are injured, which results in the leaking of proteinaceous fluid and blood into the alveolar airspace. Other findings may include alveolar hemorrhage, pulmonary capillary congestion, interstitial edema, and hyaline membrane formation. None of these changes are specific to the disease. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology -- Fetal Lung Development
retrieved_docs.content: The canalicular stage, beginning the 16th week and ending around the 25th week, marks the beginning of the development of the pulmonary acinus, the formation of a blood-air barrier, and surfactant production via type 2 cells, culminating in a lung potentially viable for gas exchange. The increasing number and size of capillaries continue to vascularize the mesenchyme. Together with the growth of the bronchioles, this thins the mesenchymal space between the respiratory epithelial basement membranes and the vascular epithelium. Eventually, these capillary and respiratory epithelial basement membranes fuse, forming a rudimentary blood-air barrier. [3] At 20 weeks, lamellar bodies begin to form in the cytoplasm of the glycogen-laden cuboidal epithelium of the bronchioles, and these cells differentiate into type 2 cells, which are capable of producing surfactant.

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheomalacia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea connects the larynx to the distal airways, and its structural support derives from 16 to 20 rings of hyaline cartilage. The rings form a "U" shape as they are incomplete at the posterior aspect, which is covered by a posterior membrane. A variety of disease processes can disrupt this support structure, causing narrowing of the lumen of the trachea (reduced anterior-posterior airway caliber) with associated loss of the semicircular shape and bulging of the posterior membranous wall. This narrowing of the trachea causes the symptoms experienced by patients. Under normal conditions, a modest expiratory collapse of the trachea constitutes a condition of frequent occurrence; in the infant, during forced expiration, a reduction of the tracheal lumen can be achieved up to 30% of its diameter. A tracheal collapse capable of producing an obstacle to lung ventilation must be greater than 50% of the tracheal diameter. In these cases, clinically evident expression of tracheomalacia occurs. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Potter Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathology study of lungs shows hypoplasia with inadequate parenchymal development, and in many cases, the respiratory tract and alveoli are lined by cuboidal epithelium. But some cases have no histopathological abnormalities. [1] [3] Hyaline membrane formation is prominent in respiratory distress syndrome. [8] Histopathology of gonads reveals hypoplastic gonads with undeveloped seminiferous tubules. [3] Microscopy of renal tissue may show primitive glomeruli, cysts formation with cuboidal or squamous epithelium lining with fluids within dilated cysts. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Lung -- Clinical Significance -- Infant Respiratory Distress
retrieved_docs.content: Infant respiratory distress is the leading cause of death in premature babies. [37] Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant starting around 20 weeks of gestation, but its full secretion does not occur until nearly 30 weeks of pregnancy. Without ample surfactant, premature infants cannot overcome the collapsing surface tension in the respiratory alveoli. When a patient goes into premature labor, Clinicians administer glucocorticoids to help prevent infant respiratory distress. [38] Glucocorticoids stimulate surfactant production in the fetus and may increase its production enough to help the infant overcome any potential respiratory distress. Testing for fetal lung maturity may be performed on the amniotic fluid by several methods, including the measurement of phospholipids in amniotic fluid (phosphatidylglycerol or lecithin-sphingomyelin ratio) and lamellar body counts.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Infants with cyanotic congenital heart disease may have similar symptoms clinically, but will not have the diffuse reticulo-granular ground glass appearance on chest radiography. The radiological findings depend on the underlying anatomic abnormality.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Specific risk populations show significantly higher incidence rates. For example, results from a prospective study by Winn et al in the United States found an incidence of 12.9% in pregnancies complicated by midtrimester rupture of membranes between 15 and 28 weeks of gestation. The study also highlighted the high mortality associated with pulmonary hypoplasia in this group, with a death rate of 95.2% in affected neonates compared to 48.2% in those without hypoplasia. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Oligohydramnios decreases the distensibility of the lungs by reducing the fluid around the potential airspaces. Severe hypoplasia is associated with reduced alveolar size and number with a proportional decrease in the pulmonary vasculature. [15] Factors such as early gestational age at rupture of membranes, a latency period of more than 6 days, and amniotic fluid index less than 1 to 2 cm (single deepest cord-free pocket of amniotic fluid) are independent risk factors for pulmonary hypoplasia in neonates with premature rupture of membranes. Pulmonary hypoplasia is an important nonrenal feature of Potter syndrome. In Potter syndrome, oligohydramnios is due to renal agenesis or outflow tract abnormality with a decrease in fetal urine excretion into the amniotic space. The oligohydramnios leads to prolonged fetal lung compression with subsequent hypoplasia. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Antenatal Management
retrieved_docs.content: Amnioinfusion Results from a study conducted by Locatelli et al in 49 patients with premature rupture of membranes at less than 26 weeks of gestation demonstrated that serial amnioinfusions reduce perinatal complications and prolong pregnancy. [40] Results from the amnioinfusion in preterm premature rupture of membranes (AMIPROM) pilot study conducted on 56 patients found no statistically significant differences in fetal or maternal outcomes. More extensive studies are needed to ascertain its benefits. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Chest Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: Chest radiography findings pathognomonic of RDS include homogenous lung disease with diffuse atelectasis, classically described as having a ground-glass reticulo-granular appearance with air bronchograms, as well as low lung volumes. The air-tissue interface formed between microalveolar collapse in the background with the air-filled larger airways in the foreground creates the classic appearance of air bronchograms.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Antenatal Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids These are administered to promote fetal lung maturation in pregnancies beyond 24 weeks of gestation, especially in cases of PPROM. [35] A lecithin-to-sphingomyelin ratio greater than 2 in amniotic fluid suggests a reduced risk of neonatal distress. [36] Antibiotics, tocolytics, and corticosteroids are frequently administered in cases of PPROM to prevent infection and prolong pregnancy. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology -- Fetal Lung Development
retrieved_docs.content: At 32 weeks, the alveolar stage begins, and alveoli start to form as the respiratory bronchioles develop septations, increasing the surface area for gas exchange. By term age, the lung contains 50 to 150 million alveoli.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Evaluation -- Pathological and Histological Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Pathological criteria Criteria include a lung weight less than 60% of predicted, RAC less than 4.1%, or LW:BW less than 0.015 (infants  younger than 28 weeks) or less than 0.012 (infants older than 28 weeks) Alternative metrics include alveolar count per unit volume, reduced airway branching, or lung deoxyribonucleic acid content less than 100 mg/kg body weight. Microscopic findings This includes a reduced number of lung cells, fewer bronchial branches, immature epithelial cells, thickened pulmonary vessels, and low surfactant concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Respiratory Epithelium -- Structure -- The Conducting Portion
retrieved_docs.content: In the most proximal airway, hyaline cartilage rings support the larger respiratory passages, namely, the trachea and bronchi, to facilitate the passage of air. Three major cell types are found in this region: ciliated, non-ciliated secretory cells, and basal cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: The pleura, the serous membrane that envelops the lungs, is classified as a component of the broader category of اضطراب جنبي (lateral disorder). This hierarchical relationship indicates that pleural conditions are considered within the spectrum of disorders affecting the lateral aspects of the thoracic cavity, underscoring the pleura’s relevance in the diagnosis and management of side‑related thoracic pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2215
retrieved_docs.content: Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is a diagnostic category that groups together various disorders affecting the amniotic cavity, and it specifically includes the condition تمزق الأغشية الباكر (early membrane rupture) as a child entity within this classification. By classifying polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity abnormalities under a single umbrella, clinicians can recognize that early membrane rupture is considered part of the broader spectrum of amniotic cavity problems, which aids in comprehensive obstetric evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity includes specific obstetric conditions such as تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) and عدوى في الجوف السلوي (infection of the amniotic cavity). This grouping indicates that disorders involving abnormal amniotic fluid volume, membrane integrity, and intra‑amniotic infection are interrelated and considered subtypes of the broader polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity problems, guiding clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of amniotic cavity and membrane disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2442
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as belonging to the diagnostic category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is used to group and define conditions such as polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity abnormalities, providing a framework for clinicians to identify, assess, and manage these specific obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 659
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity, indicating that polyhydramnios belongs to the broader category of amniotic cavity and membrane disorders. Within Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity, the specific condition تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) is listed as a child entity. Premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) is clinically associated with low transverse cesarean section, suggesting that this type of early membrane rupture often influences the decision to perform a low transverse cesarean delivery. Recognizing that premature rupture of membranes is a subcategory of polyhydramnios‑related amniotic problems and its association with low transverse cesarean section aids clinicians in diagnosing, managing, and planning appropriate obstetric care for patients with these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational age, birth weight, fetal growth, and respiratory complications with their appropriate diagnostic categories across ICD‑10‑AM, WHO perinatal codes, and multilingual clinical terminologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة” (neonatal disorder) serves as a parent concept for several specific conditions, including “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية” (fetal erythroblastosis) and “رضح الولادة” (premature birth). “Respiratory distress syndrome” is classified as a subtype of this neonatal disorder through an inverse‑isa relationship, and it is also linked as a child of “اِضطِراب الرِّئَة” (lung disorder), indicating that it is a specific form of lung pathology that occurs in the newborn period. Epidemiology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for each of these entities—“اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة,” “respiratory distress syndrome,” “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية,” and “رضح الولادة”—meaning that population‑level data and epidemiologic characteristics are relevant and can be attached to all of them. This interconnected framework highlights how neonatal respiratory conditions, fetal hematologic abnormalities, and premature delivery are related under the broader umbrella of neonatal disorders, with respiratory distress syndrome bridging both neonatal and pulmonary disease classifications, and underscores the importance of epidemiologic information in understanding and managing each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is an obstetric classification that includes تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) and عدوى في الجوف السلوي (infection of the pleural cavity) as its child conditions. تمزق الأغشية الباكر is clinically associated with low transverse cesarean section, indicating that this surgical approach may be related to or influenced by premature membrane rupture. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity also classifies itself, underscoring its role as a distinct diagnostic group within maternal‑fetal medicine. Recognizing the connections among polyhydramnios, premature rupture of membranes, associated infections, and the type of cesarean delivery helps clinicians anticipate complications and optimize perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2035
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity. The category polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity includes the specific condition تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) as a child entity, indicating that early membrane rupture is considered one of the particular disorders encompassed by polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity complications. The classification system also uses the label polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity to define itself, reinforcing the grouping of these interconnected obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 780
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is categorized under the diagnostic group Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is further classified as البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي, indicating an abnormal pleural aspiration condition. Within this category, the specific condition عدوى في الجوف السلوي (infection of the pleural cavity) is listed as a child entity, reflecting that infections of the pleural space are considered a subset of the broader Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The classification structure also notes that Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity serves as its own defining label, reinforcing its role as a primary diagnostic category.
